verbatim_text: The setting here is that we want to train LLMs to solve hard Olympiad-level math problems. Now if you take a pre-train LLM, a four-billion-parameter model, it's a pretty small-sized model that barely makes a dent on this hard problem. It's barely there. It's about 3%. Now if you take a frontier LLM, which is Gemini 3 Pro in this case, that gets to a performance of about 60% on this hard task. Now you can take this small model and try to improve it more generally using a barrage of techniques. You can use brute force RL, pick your favorite one. You can try to improve its performance. And that gets to a performance of about 34% on this task. That's pretty reasonable, but still on the level of Gemini. So now comes the interesting part. When we take this small four-b-size pre-train model and we train it with algorithm learning techniques that we'll see very shortly, then what we get is this model QEDnano. It's a four-b-size model that can scale the number of operations it performs at test time. That is, it can scale the tokens it produces.
